content: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to ideal MOSFET-based DCcoupled single-stage amplifiers having finite load resistors?

options.content: The common-gate amplifier has an infinite input resistance
The common-source amplifier has an infinite input resistance
The input and output voltages of the common-source amplifier are in phase
The input and output voltages of the common-drain amplifier are in phase

explanation:
The common-source amplifier has an infinite input resistance

The input and output voltages of the common-drain amplifier are in phase

content: In the circuit below, assume that the long channel NMOS transistor is biased in saturation. The small signal trans-conductance of the transistor is $g_m$. Neglect body effect, channel length modulation, and intrinsic device capacitances. The small signal input impedance $Z_{in}(j\omega)$ is _______
explanation:
Given : The circuit of long channel NMOS transistor is shown in figure below,

(i) NMOS transistor is biased in saturation region.

(ii) Body effect, channel length modulation and intrinsic device capacitance are negligible.

(iii) Small signal transconductance of the transistor is $g_m$.

AC small signal equivalent model for the given NMOS circuit.

For calculation of $Z_{\text {in }}(j \omega)$ applying a voltage source $V_i$ and current from it is $I_i$.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { From figure, }\\ &V_1=I_i Z_{C_1} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Applying KVL in the loop, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & V_i-I_i Z_{C_1}-\left(I_i+g_m V_1\right) Z_{C_L}=0 \\ & V_i=I_i Z_{C_1}+\left(I_i+g_m V_1\right) Z_{C_L} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Substituting the value of } V_1 \text { from equation (i), }\\ &\begin{aligned} & V_i=I_i Z_{C_1}+\left(I_i+g_m I_i Z_{C_1}\right) Z_{C_L} \\ & V_i=I_i Z_{C_1}+I_i Z_{C_L}+I_i g_m Z_{C_1} Z_{C_L} \\ & \frac{V_i}{I_i}=Z_{C_1}+Z_{C_L}+g_m Z_{C_1} Z_{C_L} \\ & Z_{i n}=Z_{C_1}+Z_{C_L}+g_m Z_{C_1} Z_{C_L} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}Now, \,\,\,\,& Z_{C_1}=\frac{1}{j \omega C_1} \\ & Z_{C_L}=\frac{1}{j \omega C_L} \end{aligned} $$

From equation (iii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & Z_{i n}=\frac{1}{j \omega C_1}+\frac{1}{j \omega C_L}+g_m \frac{1}{j \omega C_1} \frac{1}{j \omega C_L} \\ & Z_{i n}=\frac{1}{j \omega C_1}+\frac{1}{j \omega C_L}-\frac{g_m}{\omega^2 C_1 C_L} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: In the circuit shown below, $$V_1$$ and $$V_2$$ are bias voltages. Based on input and output impedances, the circuit behaves as a
explanation:
Given circuit acts as a common gate amplifier. Common gate amplifier has low input impedance and high output impedance

$$ \therefore $$ The given circuit acts as a current controlled current source.

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: Consider the CMOS circuit shown in the figure (substrates are connected to their respective sources). The gate width (W) to gate length (L) ratios $$\left( {{W \over L}} \right)$$ of the transistors are as shown. Both the transistors have the same gate oxide capacitance per unit area. For the pMOSFET, the threshold voltage is $$-$$1 V and the mobility of holes is $$40{{c{m^2}} \over {V.s}}$$. For the nMOSFET, the threshold voltage is 1 V and the mobility of electrons is $$300{{c{m^2}} \over {V.s}}$$. The steady state output voltage V0 is ___________.
explanation:
Both MOSFETs are in saturation because drain is shorted to Gate.

$${I_{DSN}} = {I_{SDP}}$$

$${{{\mu _n}{C_{ox}}} \over 2} \times {\left( {{W \over L}} \right)_N}{({V_{GSN}} - {V_{TN}})^2} = {{{\mu _p}{C_{ox}}} \over 2}{\left( {{W \over L}} \right)_P}{({V_{SGP}} - |{V_{TP}}|)^2}$$

$$300 \times 1{({V_0} - 1)^2} = 40 \times 5{(4 - {V_0} - 1)^2}$$

$$3(V_0^2 + 1 - 2{V_0}) = 2(9 + V_0^2 - 6{V_0})$$

$$ \Rightarrow V_0^2 + 6{V_0} - 15 = 0$$

$${V_0} = {{ - 6 \pm \sqrt {36 + 4 \times 15} } \over 2} = {{ - 6 \pm \sqrt {96} } \over 2}$$

V0 = 1.898 V, $$-$$7.89 V

V0 cannot be negative because V0 should lie between 0 and 4 V.

$$\therefore$$ V0 = 1.898 V

content: The ideal long channel nMOSFET and pMOSFET devices shown in the circuits have threshold voltages of 1 V and $$-$$1 V, respectively. The MOSFET substrates are connected to their respectively sources. Ignore leakage currents and assume that the capacitors are initially discharged. For the applied voltages as shown, the steady state voltages are ____________.

For the transistor $M_1$ in the circuit shown in the figure, $\mu_n C_{o x}=100 \mu \mathrm{~A} / V^2$ and $\frac{W}{L}=10$, where $\mu_n$ is the mobility of electron, $C_{o x}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area. $W$ is the width and $L$ is the length.

The channel length modulation coefficient is ignored. If the gate-to-source voltage $V_{G S}$ is 1 V to keep the transistor at the edge of saturation. Then the threshold voltage of the transistor (rounded off to one decimal place) is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
explanation:
$$ \text { From the mentioned data in the question we have } $$

$$ \begin{array}{lrl} & \mu_n C_{o x}=100 \mu \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{V}^2 \\ \text { And } & V_{G S}=1 \mathrm{~V} \\ \text { Also, } & \frac{W}{L}=10 \end{array} $$

$$ \text { The above mentioned transistor is present in saturation region } $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \text { So, } & V_{D S} \geq V_{G S}-V_T \\ \text { or, } & V_{D S}=V_{G S}-V_T \Rightarrow \text { saturation region } \\ \text { or, } & V_{D S}=1-V_T \end{array} $$

Also, $$ I_D=\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{o x}\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)\left[V_{G S}-V_T\right]^2 $$

or, $$ I_D=\left(\frac{3-V_{D S}}{20}\right) \mathrm{mA} $$

From above we have

$$ \left(\frac{3-1+V_T}{20}\right) \mathrm{mA}=\frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 10\left[1-V_T\right]^2 $$

or, $$ \left(2+V_T\right)=10\left[1+V_T^2-2 V_T\right] $$

 $$ \begin{gathered}or, 0 V_T^2-21 V_T+8=0 \\ V_T=1.6 \text { and } 0.5 \end{gathered} $$

But valid $$ V_T=0.5 \mathrm{~V} $$

Hence, the correct answer is ( 0.5 to 0.5 ).

content:
In the circuit shown in the figure, the transistors $M_1$ and $M_2$ are operating in saturation. The channel length

modulation coefficients of both the transistors are non-zero. The transconductance of the MOSFETs $M_1$ and $M_2$ are $g_{m 1}$ and $g_{m 2}$, respectively, and the internal resistance of the MOSFETs $M_1$ and $M_2$ are $r_{01}$ and $r_{02}$ respectively.

Ignoring the body effect, the ac small signal voltage gain ( $d V_{\text {out }} / d V_{\text {in }}$ ) of the circuit is
explanation:
From the question we have that MOSFET $M_2$ acting as common source amplifier.

For having MOSFET $M_1$ acts as load, Following is the Drain to gate connect.

Then we have $\quad R_{\text {eg }}=\frac{1}{g_{m 1}} \| r_{01}$

Also, $$ \frac{V_{\text {out }}}{V_{\text {in }}}=\frac{-g_{m 2} V_{g s}\left(r_{02} \| R_{e g}\right)}{V_{g s}} $$

Then we have $|A V|=-g_{m 2}\left[r_{02}\left\|\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}\right\| r_{01}\right]$

content: In the MOSFET amplifier of the figure the signal output V1 and V2 obey the relationship
An n-channel JFET has I<sub>DSS</sub> = 1 mA and V<sub>p</sub> = -5 V. Its maximum transconductance is ______
The transit time of a current carriers through the channel of an FET decides its ____________characteristics.

The identical MOSFETs $M_1$ and $M_2$ in the circuit given below are ideal and biased in the saturation region. $M_1$ and $M_2$ have a transconductance $g_m$ of 5 mS .

The input signals (in Volts) are:

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_1=2.5+0.01 \sin \omega t \\ & V_2=2.5-0.01 \sin \omega t \end{aligned} $$

The output signal $V_3$ (in Volts) is _ .
explanation:
In the circuit below, consisting of identical MOSFETs $M_1$ and $M_2$, both transistors operate ideally in the saturation region and possess a transconductance, $g_m$, of 5 mS.

The input voltages are defined as follows:

$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= 2.5 + 0.01 \sin \omega t \\ V_2 &= 2.5 - 0.01 \sin \omega t \end{aligned} $

We are tasked with determining the output signal $V_3$.

Analysis

Drain Current Calculation:

$ I_{DS} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ mA} $

Differential Mode Voltage:

$ \frac{V_d}{2} = 0.01 \sin \omega t \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_d = 0.02 \sin \omega t $

Common Mode Voltage:

$ V_{CM} = 2.5 \text{ V} $

Differential Mode Gain:

$ A_{DM} = \frac{V_0}{V_d} = -\frac{g_m \times R_D}{2} = -\frac{5 \times 1}{2} = -2.5 $

Output Voltage Due to Differential Mode:

$ V_0 = -2.5 \times V_d = -2.5 \times 0.02 \sin \omega t $

Thus, the AC component of the output voltage is:

$ V_0 = -0.05 \sin \omega t $

DC Component of the Output Voltage:

$ V_0 = 5 - 1 \times I_{DS} = 4 \text{ V} $

Common Mode Gain:

$ A_{CM} = 0 $

The common mode input effect is neutralized in this circuit.

Hence, the overall output voltage is a combination of the AC and DC components:

$ V_0 = 4 - 0.05 \sin \omega t $

content:
In the circuit shown below, the transistors $M_1$ and $M_2$ are biased in saturation. Their small signal transconductances are $g_{m1}$ and $g_{m2}$ respectively. Neglect body effect, channel length modulation and intrinsic device capacitances.

Assuming that capacitor $C_i$ is a short circuit for AC analysis, the exact magnitude of small signal voltage gain $\left| \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \right|$ is ______.
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

(i) Transistors $M_1$ and $M_2$ are biased in saturation.

(ii) Small signal transconductance for $M_1$ and $M_2$ is $g m_1$ and $g m_2$.

(iii) Body effect, channel length modulation and intrinstic device capacitances are negligible.

Small signal equivalent circuit is shown below,

$$ \text { From figure, } v_{\text {out }}=-g_{m 2} v_{g_{s_2}} R_D \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Apply voltage division rule at input }\\ &v_{g_{s_2}}=\frac{v_{i n}\left(R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}\right)}{R_S+R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii) \end{aligned} $$

From equation (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & v_{\text {out }}=-g_{m 2} R_D v_{\text {in }} \frac{\left(R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}\right)}{R_B+R_S+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}} \\ & \frac{v_{\text {out }}}{v_{\text {in }}}=-g_{m 2} R_D \frac{\left(R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}\right)}{\left(R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}+R_S\right)} \end{aligned} $$

Magnitude of small signal voltage gain

$$ \left|\frac{v_{\text {out }}}{v_{\text {in }}}\right|=g_{m 2} R_D \frac{\left(R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}\right)}{\left(R_B+\frac{1}{g_{m 1}}+R_S\right)} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content:
An NMOS transistor operating in the linear region has $I_{D}$ of 5 $\mu$A at $V_{DS}$ of 0.1 V. Keeping $V_{GS}$ constant, the $V_{DS}$ is increased to 1.5 V.

Given that $\mu_{n}C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}$ = 50 $\mu$A/$V^2$, the transconductance at the new operating point (in $\mu$A/V, rounded off to two decimal places) is ______.
explanation:
Given : NMOS operating in linear region

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_{D S}=5 \mu \mathrm{~A} \\ & V_{D S}=0.1 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mu_n C_{o x} \frac{W}{L}=50 \mu \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{V}^2 \end{aligned} $$

In linear region drain to source current is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_{D S}=\frac{\mu_n C_{o x} W}{L}\left[\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) V_{D S}-\frac{V_{D S}^2}{2}\right] \\ & 5 \times 10^{-6}=50 \times 10^{-6}\left[\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) 0.1-\frac{(0.1)^2}{2}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{10}=\left[\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) 0.1-\frac{(0.1)^2}{2}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{10}=\left[\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) 0.1-0.005\right] \\ & 0.1=\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) 0.1-0.005 \\ & 0.1+0.005=\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) 0.1 \\ & 0.105=\left(V_{G S}-V_T\right) 0.1 \\ & V_{G S}-V_T=1.05 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Now, keeping $V_{G S}$ constant, $V_{D S}$ is increased to 1.5 V

$$ \begin{aligned}i.e., &\,\, V_{D S}=1.5 \mathrm{~V} \\ & V_{G S}-V_T=1.05 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

$$Also,\,\, V_{D S}>V_{G S}-V_T $$

Hence, MOSFET operates in saturation region.

Transconductance of MOSFET working in saturation region is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} g_{m(s a t)} & =\frac{\mu_n C_{o x} W}{L}\left[V_{G S}-V_T\right] \\ & =50 \times 10^{-6}[1.05]=52.5 \mu \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{V} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the transconductance at the new operating point is $\mathbf{5 2 . 5} \boldsymbol{\mu} \mathbf{A} / \mathbf{V}$.

content: Consider an ideal long channel nMOSFET (enhancement-mode) with gate length 10 $$\mu$$m and width 100 $$\mu$$m. The product of electron mobility ($$\mu$$n) and oxide capacitance per unit area (Cox) is $$\mu$$nCox = 1 mA/V2. The threshold voltage of the transistor is 1 V. For a gate-to-source voltage VGS = [2 $$-$$ sin(2t)] V and drain-to source voltage VDS = 1 V (substrate connected to the source), the maximum value of the drain-to-source current is ___________.
explanation:
$${V_{GS}} = [2 - \sin 2t]$$ Volt

$${V_{GS\,\min }} = 2 - 1 = 1$$ V

$${V_{GS\max }} = 2 - ( - 1) = 3$$ V

$${V_{DS}} = 1$$ V

$${V_{DS}} < {V_{GS}} - {V_t}$$ (Linear)

$${I_D} = {\mu _n}{C_{ox}}{W \over L}\left\{ {({V_{GS}} - {V_t}){V_{DS}} - {1 \over 2}V_{DS}^2} \right\}$$

$${I_{D\max }} = {\mu _n}{C_{ox}}{W \over L}\left[ {({V_{GS\max }} - {V_t}){V_{DS}} - {1 \over 2}V_{DS}^2} \right]$$

= 1mA/V2 $$ \times 10\left[ {(3 - 1)1 - {1 \over 2} \times 1} \right]$$

= 10 [1.5] = 15 mA

content: Consider the circuit shown with an ideal long channel nMOSFET (enhancement mode, substrate is connected to the source). The transistor is appropriately biased in the saturation region with VGG and VDD such that it acts as a linear amplifier. vi is the small-signal ac input voltage. vA and vB represent the small-signal voltages at the nodes A and B, respectively. The value of $${{{v_A}} \over {{v_B}}}$$ is __________ (rounded off to one decimal place).
explanation:
Consider Va as an output then the small signal model is

Voltage gain $${{{v_a}} \over {{v_{in}}}} = {{ - {g_m}{R_D}} \over {1 + {g_m}{R_s}}}$$ ..... (1)

Consider Vb as an output, then the small signal model,

Voltage gain $${{{V_b}} \over {{V_{in}}}} = {{{g_m}{R_s}} \over {1 + {g_m}{R_s}}}$$ ...... (2)

On dividing equation (1) and (2)

$${{{V_a}/{V_{in}}} \over {{V_b}/{V_{in}}}} = {{ - {R_D}} \over {{R_s}}}$$

$${{{V_a}} \over {{V_b}}} = {{ - 4k} \over {2k}} = - 2$$

content:
For an $n$-channel silicon MOSFET with 10 nm gate oxide thickness, the substrate sensitivity ( $\partial V_T / \partial\left|V_{B S}\right|$ ) is found to be $50 \mathrm{mV} / \mathrm{V}$ at a substrate voltage $\left|V_{B S}\right|=2 \mathrm{~V}$, where $V_T$ is the threshold voltage of the MOSFET. Assume that, $\left|V_{B S}\right| \gg 2 \phi_B$, where $q \phi_B$ is the separation between the Femi energy level $E_F$ and the intrinsic level $E_i$ in the bulk. Parameters given are

Electron charge $(q)=1.6 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$

Vacuum permittivity $\left(\varepsilon_o\right)=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F} / \mathrm{m}$

Relative permittivity of silicon $\left(\varepsilon_{S i}\right)=12$

Relative permittivity of oxide $\left(\varepsilon_{o x}\right)=4$

The doping concentration of the substrate is
explanation:
From mentioned data in the question, we have

$$ \begin{aligned} q & =1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ \varepsilon_0 & =8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F} / \mathrm{m} \\ \varepsilon_{s i} & =12 \\ \varepsilon_{0 x} & =4 \\ t_{o x} & =10 \mathrm{~nm} \end{aligned} $$

Now we have $\frac{\partial V_T}{\partial\left|V_{B S}\right|}=50 \mathrm{mv} / \mathrm{v}$. At the condition when

$V_{B S}=2 \mathrm{~V}$

 

or,  $$ \left|V_{B S}\right| \gg 2 \varphi_B, $$

For finding the substrate's doping concentration of the substrate = ?, we have

$$ V_t=V_{t o}+\gamma\left[\sqrt{V_{B S}+\left|2 \Phi_F\right|}-\sqrt{\left|2 \Phi_F\right|}\right] $$

Also,  $$ \frac{\partial V_T}{\partial\left|V_{B S}\right|}=\frac{}{2 \sqrt{V_{B S}+\left|2_F^1{ }_F\right|}}=50 \mathrm{mv} / \mathrm{v} $$

or,  $$ \gamma=0.1 \times \sqrt{2} $$

or,  $$ \gamma=\frac{\sqrt{2 q N_A \varepsilon_{s i}}}{C_{o x}}=0.1 \times \sqrt{2} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}Also, \,\,we\,\, have \,\,C_{o x} & =\frac{\varepsilon_{\alpha x}}{t_{\alpha x}} \\ & =\frac{4 \times 8085 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-19}} \end{aligned} $$

or, $$ C_{o x}=3.54 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~F} / \mathrm{m}^2 $$

Now we have,$\quad q \cdot N_A \cdot \varepsilon_{s i}=C_{o x}^2 \times 0.01$

After putting values, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times N_A \times 12 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \\ =10^{-12} \times\left(3.54 \times 10^{-13}\right)^2 \end{aligned} $$

or,  $$ N_A=\frac{1.25316 \times 10^{-7}}{1.699 \times 10^{-29}} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}or,\,\, & N_A=7.37 \times 10^{21} / \mathrm{m}^3 \\ & N_A=7.37 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} \end{aligned} $$

content: Using the incremental low frequency small - signal model of the MOS device, the Norton equivalent resistance of the following circuit is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown in figure below, } $$

$$ \text { Small signal equivalent circuit is shown below, } $$

Applying KVL in loop 1,

$$ \begin{aligned} & -V_{g s}-V_x=0 \\ & V_{g s}=-V_x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} $$

Applying KVL in loop 2,

$$ V_x-r_{d s}\left(I_x+g_m V_{g s}\right)-R I_x=0 $$

From equation (i), putting $V_{g s}=-V_x$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & +V_x-r_{d s} I_x+g_m V_x r_{d s}-R I_x=0 \\ & V_x\left(1+g_m r_{d s}\right)=I_x\left(r_{d s}+R\right) \\ & \frac{V_x}{I_x}=R_N=\frac{r_{d s}+R}{1+g_m r_{d s}} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: An enhancement MOSFET of threshold voltage 3 V is being used in the sample and hold circuit given below. Assume that the substrate of the MOS device is connected to -10 V . If the input voltage $v_1$ liesbetween $\pm 10 \mathrm{~V}$, the minimum and the maximum value of $v_G$ required for proper sampling and holding respectively, are
explanation:
$$ \text { The given circuit is shown in below figure, } $$

For sampling the input signal, the MOSFET must be ON.

During sampling :

$$ V_G-V_1>V_{t h} \quad(\text { MOSFET }=0 \mathrm{ON}) $$

The equivalent circuit will be,

$$ \begin{aligned}Therefore, \,\,\,  & V_G-V_{i \max }>V_{t h} \\ & V_G-10>3 \\ & V_G>10+3 \\ & V_G>13 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

So, minimum value gate voltage must be 13 Volt.

For Holding :

$$ V_G-V_1 < V_{t h} $$

The equivalent circuit will be,

$$ \begin{aligned}Therefore,\,\, & V_G-V_1 < V_{t h} \\ & V_G-V_{i \min } < V_{t h} \\ & V_G-(-10)<3 \\ & V_G < 3-10 \\ & V_G < -7 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

So, maximum value is -7 V .

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: The action of a JFET in its equivalent circuit can be best represented as a

Consider the following statements in connection with the CMOS inverter in the Figure. Where both the MOSFETS are of enhancement type and both have a threshold voltage of 2V.

Statement 1: T1 conducts when Vi $$ \ge \,2\,V$$.
Statement 2: T1 is always in saturation when $${V_0}\, = \,0\,V$$.

Which of the following is correct?
The JFET in the circuit shown in fig. has an IDSS = 10mA and Vp = -5V. The value of the resistance Rs for a drain current IDS = 6.4mA is (select the Nearest value).
An  n-channel JFET has a pinch-off voltage of V<sub>p</sub> = -5V. V<sub>DS</sub>(max) = 20V and g<sub>m</sub>  = 2mA/V. The minimum 'ON' resistance is achieved in the JFET for
A JEFT with VP = -4V and IDss = 12mA is used in the circuit shown in Fig. Assuming the device to be operating in saturation, determine ID, VDS and VGS

In the JFET circuit shown in Fig assume that R1||R2 = 1 M$$\Omega $$ and the total stray capacitance at the output to be 20 pF. The JFET used has gm = 2mA/V, Cgs = 20 pF and Cgd = 2 pF. Determine the upper cut-off frequency of the amplifier.

In the MOSFET amplifier shown in the fig. below, the transistor has $$\mu \,\, = \,\,50,\,\,{r_d}\,\, = \,\,10\,K\Omega ,\,\,{C_{gs}}\,\, = \,\,5\,\,pF,{C_{gd}}\,\, = \,\,1pF\,\,$$ and $${C_{ds}}\,\, = \,\,2\,pF.$$ Draw a small signal equivalent circuit for the amplifier for midband frequencies and calculate its midband voltage gain.

(a) Specify the type of the negative feedback in the JFET amplifier shown in Fig.
(b)Calculate the voltage gain (V0/Vi), of the amplifier with no feedback (Rf=$$\infty $$).
(c)Determine the value of the resistance Rf, required to give a voltage gain (V0/Vi), or - 10.
answer:
(a) This is a voltage shunt feedback Amplifier.
(b)voltage gain = -454
(c) Rf = 10 K$$\Omega $$
